summary:
Charles V (1500–1558 AD) ruled the greatest European empire since Rome — simultaneously King of Spain, Holy Roman Emperor, Lord of the Netherlands, ruler of southern Italy, and sovereign of the Spanish Americas — yet spent his entire reign exhausted by simultaneous crises: the Ottoman advance, the Protestant Reformation, French rivalry, and colonial governance. Inheriting his vast domains through four separate dynastic accidents before the age of 20, Charles spent most of his 40-year reign in constant motion, traveling between Spain, the Netherlands, Italy, and Germany without ever establishing a permanent capital. He faced the Ottoman Empire under [[event:2401|Suleiman the Magnificent]] in Hungary and the Mediterranean; fought four major wars against Francis I of France; confronted [[event:2430|Luther's]] Reformation and the religious disintegration of Germany; and oversaw the [[event:2319|conquest of the Aztec Empire]] and the [[event:2320|conquest of the Inca Empire]] under his conquistadors.

Charles's enormous power masked fundamental limitations: his empire lacked a unified capital, administration, or language; his finances were perpetually overstretched despite the flood of New World silver; and his enemies were too numerous to be defeated simultaneously. He achieved significant victories — the Diet of Worms suppressed Luther (temporarily), Tunis was taken from the Ottomans (temporarily), and French ambitions in Italy were checked at Pavia (1525) — but could not achieve decisive outcomes on any front. Exhausted and disillusioned, he abdicated his various thrones between 1555 and 1556, dividing the empire between his son Philip II (Spain, Netherlands, Italy, Americas) and his brother Ferdinand I (Austria, Holy Roman Empire) — a split that would define European politics for the next century. He died in a monastery in 1558, having famously employed multiple clocks whose synchronization occupied his retirement.
